talk_title: CQ0736 polysaccharide‑based KRAS‑targeted therapy via macropinocytosis

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant lung, pancreatic and colorectal CDX/PDX models

MOA_summary: CQ0736 is a dextran‑based conjugate bearing docetaxel and gamma‑linolenic acid that exploits enhanced macropinocytosis in KRAS‑driven tumors for selective tumor accumulation; the conjugate releases docetaxel intracellularly, inducing apoptosis while avoiding P‑gp efflux, resulting in durable tumor regression across KRAS G12C, G12D and wild‑type models.
key_findings: CQ0736 achieved sustained intratumoral docetaxel exposure for 96 h versus free DTX.
In KRAS G12C lung CDX, weekly CQ0736 reduced tumor volume 3.6‑fold vs RMC and 5.2‑fold vs adagrasib at day 28.
CQ0736 showed durable tumor control without resistance in KRAS G12C PDX (16.5‑fold reduction vs RMC at day 49).
No significant body‑weight loss or hematologic toxicity observed across CDX/PDX studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Reduced P‑gp mediated efflux via gamma‑linolenic acid
Macropinocytosis‑mediated uptake 5‑10 × higher in KRAS‑mutant vs wild‑type cells
open_questions: What is the exact half‑life of CQ0736 in humans?
Can the polysaccharide platform be adapted to other cytotoxic payloads?

talk_title: Orally bioavailable small‑molecule MYC translation inhibitors (WDC‑623)

biomarkers.name: c-MYC protein level
biomarkers.finding: Reduced >100‑fold potency to single‑digit nM (WDC‑623) versus ~1 µM for hit

clinical_data.value: significant inhibition at 5 mg/kg BID oral

MOA_summary: WDC‑623 binds to a regulatory element in the MYC 5'‑UTR, stabilizing an inhibitory RNA structure that blocks translation initiation; rapid loss of MYC protein (half‑life ~30 min) leads to selective repression of MYC‑driven transcriptional programs while sparing non‑MYC short‑half‑life proteins.
key_findings: Structure‑guided hit‑to‑lead improved potency >100‑fold to single‑digit nM (WDC‑623).
WDC‑623 reduced c‑MYC protein within 30 min without affecting MYC mRNA levels.
Proteomics showed c‑MYC and its direct targets CDC25A/B were the most repressed proteins after 2 h treatment.
Oral BID dosing (5 mg/kg) produced significant tumor growth inhibition in SCLC CDX and 125 % TGI in PDAC xenograft with no weight loss.
Compound activity is nutrient‑dependent; high nutrient levels (normal plasma) blunt MYC repression, suggesting tumor‑selective activity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by nutrient‑dependent translation control; minimal effect on L‑MYC or other short‑half‑life proteins.
open_questions: Can the nutrient‑dependent mechanism be leveraged to improve therapeutic window in patients?
What is the chronic safety profile of sustained MYC translation inhibition?

talk_title: Intramolecular glue Cbl‑B inhibitors (NX‑1607) advancing to Phase 1

biomarkers.name: phospho‑HS1
biomarkers.finding: dose‑dependent increase correlating with efficacy in mouse models

clinical_data.value: robust efficacy at 10–30 mg/kg daily in mouse models
clinical_data.population: CT26, TC1, TNBC, B‑cell lymphoma models

clinical_data.value: enhanced survival in multiple tumor models

MOA_summary: NX‑1607 binds to the closed conformation pocket at the interface of Cbl‑B’s three domains, acting as an intramolecular glue that locks Cbl‑B in an inactive state, thereby releasing the brake on T‑cell and NK‑cell activation; the resulting immune activation synergizes with anti‑PD‑1 therapy to produce tumor regression.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 250 k compounds yielded a single Cbl‑B hit (NRX‑001) with 30 µM potency.
Enantiomeric resolution identified the active S‑enantiomer; crystal structure revealed binding to a buried pocket near Tyr363.
Medicinal chemistry optimization produced NX‑1607 with sub‑nanomolar Kd (0.4 nM) and excellent oral bioavailability.
NX‑1607 monotherapy achieved tumor regression at 10–30 mg/kg daily in colorectal, TNBC, and B‑cell lymphoma models.
Combination with anti‑PD‑1 showed synergistic survival benefit across multiple syngeneic models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity for Cbl‑B over other E3 ligases demonstrated by lack of Src inhibition in primary screen.
No activity observed in Cbl‑B knockout mice, confirming on‑target mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Cbl‑B inhibition releases T‑cell brake, enhancing checkpoint blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant survival extension vs anti‑PD‑1 alone

open_questions: Can NX‑1607 be combined safely with other immune agonists (e.g., CD40 agonists)?
What is the long‑term safety of chronic Cbl‑B inhibition in humans?

talk_title: NEO811, a first‑in‑class ARNT molecular glue degrader for clear cell RCC

clinical_data.value: significant regression after 21 days daily dosing
minimal degradation of SALL4, CK1α, Ikaros compared with earlier hits

MOA_summary: NEO811 binds cereblon and creates a neo‑substrate interface that recruits ARNT, inducing a conformational rearrangement that enables ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation; loss of ARNT disrupts HIF‑α heterodimer transcription, leading to potent anti‑tumor activity in VHL‑deficient RCC.
key_findings: Structure‑guided design converted initial hit (compound A) into selective ARNT degrader (compound C) with reduced off‑target cereblon neo‑substrate degradation.
NEO811 demonstrated >80 % ARNT degradation at low nanomolar concentrations in cellular assays.
In vivo, once‑daily oral dosing for 21 days produced marked tumor regression in ccRCC xenografts with good tolerability.
NEO811 showed superior potency to FDA‑approved HIF‑2α inhibitor belzutifan in HIF transcriptional inhibition assays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Off‑target degradation of SALL4, CK1α, Ikaros mitigated by steric optimization of cereblon‑binding moiety.
open_questions: Will ARNT degradation overcome resistance to current VEGF/HIF‑2α inhibitors?
What is the safety profile of chronic ARNT loss in normal tissues?

talk_title: DNA‑templated spatially controlled degraders (DTAG) for CDK4/6‑Cyclin D1

clinical_data.comparator: DNA alone, ligand‑only, other DTAG versions

MOA_summary: DTAG‑v1 uses a 20‑bp double‑stranded DNA scaffold to position a CDK4/6 inhibitor and an E3 ligase recruiter at defined distances; the optimal geometry (ligand on major groove) enables formation of a productive ternary complex that simultaneously degrades CDK4, CDK6 and the undruggable Cyclin D1, demonstrating that spatial programming via DNA can control target selectivity.
readouts: Western blot of CDK4/6 and Cyclin D1
key_findings: Four DNA‑templated PROTAC geometries showed distinct degradation profiles; version 1 (major‑groove placement) degraded CDK4, CDK6 and Cyclin D1.
Molecular dynamics revealed that productive ternary complexes correlated with negative ΔG values and close proximity of CDK4/6 to the E3 ligase.
In vivo, DTAG‑v1 achieved robust tumor regression in U251 xenografts with no significant body‑weight loss.
The platform is modular: swapping the CDK4/6 ligand for CDK9 or CDK2 inhibitors generated degraders for other cyclin‑CDK complexes without re‑synthesizing the DNA scaffold.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by DNA‑defined geometry; larger spacer (version 3) failed to induce degradation.
open_questions: Can DTAG be adapted for systemic delivery and nuclease stability in vivo?
Will DNA‑based degraders trigger innate immune pathways (cGAS‑STING)?

talk_title: BH‑501284, a pseudo‑irreversible pan‑KRAS switch‑II inhibitor

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant (G12V, G12D, G12C) cell lines and CDX models

biomarkers.name: p‑ERK
biomarkers.finding: sustained inhibition after washout (90 % retained at 24 h)

clinical_data.population: KRAS mutant solid tumor cell lines

clinical_data.value: deep regression at 5 mg/kg BID in H358 CDX
59 % TGI at 100 mg/kg BID for AMG 410 comparator

MOA_summary: BH‑501284 binds the KRAS switch‑II pocket in both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound states with picomolar affinity and a residence time >54 h, rigidifying KRAS and preventing RAF interaction; the pseudo‑irreversible kinetic profile yields sustained pathway inhibition after drug washout.
key_findings: SPR showed picomolar Kd and >54 h residence time on GDP‑bound KRAS G12D, outperforming AMG 410 (1.4 h).
Washout experiments demonstrated >90 % p‑ERK inhibition retained 24 h after removal, comparable to covalent G12C inhibitor.
Potent activity across KRAS G12V, G12D, G12C (IC50 <3 nM) and KRAS‑amplified lines (IC50 ~1.5 nM); inactive on NRAS/HRAS mutants.
Oral BID dosing as low as 5 mg/kg achieved deep tumor regressions in multiple KRAS mutant CDX models.
Only ~2‑fold shift in IC50 when EGFR ligand added, and synergistic with cetuximab in long‑term growth assays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity over NRAS/HRAS confirmed; modest activity on KRAS G12R absent.
Pseudo‑irreversible binding may mitigate rapid resistance emergence.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR‑mediated re‑activation of KRAS signaling is a known resistance mechanism
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic inhibition of long‑term cell growth

open_questions: Will the long residence time translate to favorable human PK/PD?
Can BH‑501284 overcome acquired resistance to covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors?

talk_title: Proteomics‑guided discovery of TRIM21‑mediated multimeric degradation (ACE compounds)
MOA_summary: ACE compounds act as molecular glues that convert the E3 ligase TRIM21 into a degrader of nuclear pore complex proteins; binding is NUP98‑dependent, leading to ordered degradation of inner‑ring NPC subunits followed by outer‑ring components, demonstrating that multimeric assembly state governs TRIM21‑mediated proteasomal clearance.
key_findings: Phenotypic screen identified three ACE compounds that cause IFN‑γ‑dependent cell death.
CRISPR screen highlighted TRIM21 as essential for ACE activity.
Proteomics revealed selective degradation of inner‑ring NPC proteins, reversible by proteasome inhibition.
Time‑course proteomics showed hierarchical degradation: inner‑ring NPC subunits degraded within 4 h, outer‑ring later, nuclear basket unchanged at 12 h.
BioID proximity labeling demonstrated ACE‑dependent recruitment of TRIM21 to NPC complexes via NUP98; NUP98 knockout abolished recruitment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity limited to multimeric NPC assemblies; monomeric proteins are spared.
Dependence on NUP98 suggests potential resistance via NUP98 loss.
open_questions: Can TRIM21‑based glues be harnessed to degrade pathogenic protein condensates in vivo?
What are the immunological consequences of chronic TRIM21 activation?
